segments.s099-g1: بزد is a simple past verb ('he struck/struck at') placed before its object as is common in classical Persian. مهره در جام is a prepositional-phrase object: مهره ('bead/amulet') governed by در جام ('in the cup'), and بر پشت پیل ('on the elephant's back') is a location phrase attached after the object to specify where the striking happened.
segments.s099-g2: ازو is a short compound pronoun meaning 'from it/from him' and here links the sound to the struck object; برشد is a (classical/poetic) intransitive perfective/preterite meaning 'it rose/issued forth' and takes آواز ('sound/voice') as its subject; تا چند میل is a measure phrase where تا means 'as far as' and چند میل indicates extent ('several miles'), placed after the verb phrase to indicate how far the sound carried.
segments.s099-g3: خروشیدن is a verbal noun (infinitive used as a noun) meaning 'the roaring/clamor'; کوس is the noun subject ('war-drum') and با کرنای is an instrumental phrase ('with the trumpet' or 'accompanied by a trumpet-blast'), with با marking accompaniment.
segments.s099-g4: همان is a demonstrative ('that/the same') pointing back to what was just described; ژنده modifies پیلان ('ragged elephants') in an attributive fashion, and و هندی درای links another coordinated phrase ('and the Indian ... درای'). درای here is an older form meaning 'possessing'/'having' or 'wealthy', used adjectivally after هندی; the whole segment lists the parties producing the noise.
segments.s099-g5: برآمد is a past intransitive verb meaning 'arose/issued forth'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; زاولستان is the place-name 'Zabulistan'; رستخیز here denotes a mass/event ('uprising' or 'resurrection') and functions as the thing that 'arose' from that place.
segments.s099-g6: زمین خفته را is a marked object/topic phrase meaning 'the sleeping earth' (زمین 'earth' modified by خفته 'asleep', with را marking it); بانگ is 'a cry/sound' and would be the thing issued, while برزد is a poetic verbal form meaning 'struck/uttered' (here used with بانگ). که introduces the quoted imperative خیز ('Rise!'), which is an address/command to the sleeping earth.
segments.s099-g7: به پیش اندرون is a compound locative phrase meaning 'forward into the interior/inner quarters' (به پیش = forward/toward the front; اندرون = inside). رستم پهلوان is a nominative apposition 'Rostam the champion' and is the agent associated with the motion implied by the preceding phrase.
segments.s099-g8: پس پشت او here means 'behind him' (پس = after/behind; پشت = back), and سالخورده گوان is a descriptive phrase: سالخورده 'aged/old' with گوان used adverbially or adjectivally ('like/so'), so the phrase characterizes those following him as 'old-like' or 'as if aged.' The verb of motion is implied from the previous clause.
segments.s099-g9: چنان شد is a fixed poetic sequence meaning 'it became so' or 'such was the result' (چنان + شد, where شد is past). ز introduces the source ('from' / 'because of') and links to لشکر ('the army'). در و دشت و راغ is a simple list of locations joined by و; در here is the substantive 'gate(s)/towns' rather than a preposition. The whole segment sets up the cause/scene for the result clause that follows.
segments.s099-g10: که introduces a result clause ('so that / so that it happened that'). بر سر is a locative phrase 'on (one's) head' with بر as the preposition and سر the noun. نیارست here is part of a negative/resultive construction meaning 'could not alight/settle' (the precise lexeme is archaic/poetic; none of the provided senses fit exactly). پرید is the simple past 'flew/jumped' (3rd person), and زاغ is the subject 'the crow/rook.' The clause therefore expresses the resulting impossibility of the bird landing.
segments.s099-g11: زدندی is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural past 'they struck/they smote' (the -ندی ending). تبیره is the instrument or object ('with (an) axe/club' in context of battle) and functions as the thing they used; here none of the provided senses matches perfectly. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (continuative/iterative 'thus/ever'), and شست is the past of 'to wash' used metaphorically with جای ('place') to mean 'swept away' or 'cleared the place.'
segments.s099-g12: جهان را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the world' as the domain being affected). نه ... نه ... is the coordinating negation 'neither ... nor ...'. بود is the past copula 'was,' and پیدا is a postpositive adjective/adverb meaning 'visible' or 'apparent.' Grammatically the order 'نه سر بود پیدا نه پای' reads 'neither head was visible nor (was there a) foot,' i.e. total devastation.
segments.s099-g13: به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time (of)'. بشکوفه is a verblike/adjectival participial form meaning 'in bloom' or 'when it blossomed' (بـ- prefix + شکوفه), and گلستان is the noun 'rose-garden' or 'flower-garden.' Together they form the adverbial time clause 'when the garden was in bloom.'
segments.s099-g14: بیاورد is a narrative past/present-historical form 'he brought' / 'he caused to bring' (3rd person). لشکر is the direct object 'army' and ز + زابلستان marks origin 'from Zabulistan.' Word order (verb before object) is common in this elevated/older style.
segments.s099-g15: ز indicates the source ('from' or 'concerning') and attaches to زال ('Zal') as the person associated with the news. آگهی یافت is a compound past: آگهی 'news/information' + یافت 'obtained/received' (past of یافتن), so literally 'received news.' افراسیاب is the subject who receives that information. The order places the source before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s099-g16: برآمد is the past 'he rose/rose up' or 'he came forth.' The prepositions ز / از mark departure from a state: ز آرام 'from repose/peace' and از خورد و خواب 'from eating and sleep' (خورد here = 'eating', خواب = 'sleep'). The coordinated nouns convey that he left his comfort and sustenance upon receiving the news.
segments.s099-g33: شهی is a noun meaning a royal person / king; here it introduces the subject. کاو is an archaic relative form (equivalent to که او = “who/that he”) linking this subject to the following predicate. باورنگ is a compound sense meaning “having a (particular) colour/complexion” (با + رنگ) and here is the direct object of دارد. دارد is present‑tense, third‑person singular of داشتن meaning “has/possesses.” ز is the poetic preposition “from/of,” and می is the noun “wine” — together ز می indicates the source of the colour (i.e. ‘tinged by wine’). Word order: subject → relative → predicate (object + verb) with the source phrase ز می attached to the object.
segments.s099-g34: که here heads a subordinate/relative clause “so/that …” referring back to the king. بی‌سر is an adjective meaning “headless” (بی‑ = without + سر = head). نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن (not to be) used in a subordinate/result clause here (“may not be / is not”); the form is subordinate (not an imperative). تن آدمی = “the body of man / the human body,” where تن is the noun “body” and آدمی specifies it as human. The clause states a negative condition or result about the human body in relation to the preceding description.
segments.s099-g35: نشان داد is a two‑word verbal phrase: نشان (a noun “sign, token”) combined with داد (past tense of دادن, “gave/shown”); together they mean “showed a sign” or “gave a sign.” موبد is the agent (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest); in classical word order the verb may precede its agent. مرا is the object clitic (me + object marker را split here as م + را) marking the recipient. در زمان is a prepositional phrase “in the time (of),” locating the action temporally.
segments.s099-g36: یکی شاه is an idiomatic classical ordering meaning “a certain king” (یکی = a certain / some; شاه = king). با introduces accompanying qualities: فر is the technical noun farr (royal glory), and بخت جوان is a compound meaning “young fortune” or “fresh luck” (بخت = fortune; جوان = young). The sequence uses با … و … to list the king’s attributes.
segments.s099-g37: ز تخم فریدون is a genitive/lineage phrase “from the seed/lineage of Fereydun” (ز = from; تخم = seed/offspring; فریدون = proper name). یل is an appositive noun meaning “hero” attached to the name that follows, and کیقباد is the proper name Kay Qobad; the sequence is a classical apposition ‘یل کیقباد’ = ‘the hero Kay Qobad.’
segments.s099-g38: که introduces a relative clause describing Kay Qobad (“who …”). با … و … is a repeated prepositional pattern listing qualities: با فر و برزست = “with farr (royal glory) and exaltation” (برزست here means ‘was exalted’/‘exalted state’ used as a nominalized quality), and با رای و داد = “and with counsel/wise plan and justice.” The structure is coordinate: attributes joined by و with parallel با marking.
segments.s099-g17: بیاورد is a past 3rd‑person singular form (archaic/poetic word order) of آوردن meaning “he brought.” It precedes its object لشکر (army) here; سوی + place (خوار ری) marks the direction “toward Khwar (of) Rey.” The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s099-g18: بران (contraction of بر آن) = “upon that / on it,” linking to the following noun مرغزاری (a meadow/grove) which is then qualified by the relative که “which.” بد آب و نی describes the meadow’s condition: بد = “bad/poor,” آب = “water,” و = “and,” نی here = “reed(s)” (i.e. swampy/poor water with reeds). The whole phrase tells where the army was placed and the state of that place.
segments.s099-g19: ز ایران = “from Iran” (ز is the poetic form of از). بیامد is simple past 3rd singular “came.” دمادم is an adverb “constantly/continually” modifying بیامد. سپاه (army/host) is the subject placed after the verb in this poetic inversion.
segments.s099-g20: ز راه بیابان = “from the desert road” (ز = from, راه = road/path, بیابان = desert/open country). سوی رزمگاه = “toward the battlefield” (سوی = toward, رزمگاه = place of battle). These are consecutive adverbial/place phrases giving the army’s route and destination.
segments.s099-g21: ز لشکر به لشکر = “from army to army / between the armies” using ز … به to set the endpoints. دو فرسنگ is the measured distance (“two parasangs”) and ماند (past of ماندن) means “remained / lay” — the whole clause states that a gap of two parasangs separated the two hosts.
segments.s099-g22: سپهبد = the spahbed (a military commander) is the subject. جهاندیدگان is the plural object meaning “those who have seen the world” (i.e. worldly‑wise/veterans); را is the definite object marker; بخواند (here) = “called/summoned” (an extended sense of خواندن). The clause says the spahbed summoned those experienced men.
segments.s099-g23: بدیشان = “to them” (دِ + ایشان), an indirect object marker phrase; چنین = “thus/so”; گفت is the past 3rd‑person “he said.” کای is the vocative particle (O!). بخردان is the vocative plural “wise ones.” The sequence is the reporting clause “He said to them thus: ‘O wise ones…’”.
segments.s099-g24: جهاندیده و کارکرده are two coordinated adjectives: جهاندیده = “worldly‑wise / well‑traveled,” کارکرده = “experienced / having acted (i.e. seasoned).” They modify the following noun ردان (here a plural noun in vocative — likely “riders” or “mounted men” in context). Together they form the address to those seasoned men.
segments.s099-g25: SOV order: the direct object این لشکر precedes the verb آراستم (first‑person past). هم at the front adds 'also/too' and ایدر = 'here'; من (I) appears for emphasis though the verb already marks the subject. آراستم is the past 1sg of آراییدن/آراییدن (to array/arrange).
segments.s099-g26: خواستم is past 1sg 'I wished/desired'. بسی here functions as an intensifying adverb ('much/very'); it scopes over the coordinated nouns سروری و مهی (sovereignty and a moon‑like glory). مهی is a poetic adjective ('moon‑like', i.e. radiant/beautiful) used substantially (as a quality or glory being desired).
segments.s099-g27: پراگنده شد is a passive/inchoative phrasing 'became widespread/scattered'. رای is the thing that spread ('opinion' or report). بی تخت شاه is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the king without a throne' (بی تخت = 'throneless'); word order places the descriptor after رای.
segments.s099-g28: همه کار ('all matters/affairs') is the topic; بی‌روی qualifies manner as 'without face/honor' or 'without grace' and بی‌سر ('headless') means 'without a leader'. The two adjectives are coordinated by و and apply to سپاه (the host/army), yielding 'affairs were shameless/aimless and the host was leaderless.'
segments.s099-g29: چو ... بنشست is a temporal clause 'when he sat upon the throne' (چو = 'when'). فرخنده is an epithet 'the auspicious/fortunate one'; ز + و here represents the poetic contraction زُ او (ز او) meaning 'from him / because of him'. The verb of the clause is بنشست (past 'sat').
segments.s099-g30: ز گیتی 'from the world' is an ablative phrase that licenses آفرین ('an acclamation/cheer'). یکی is 'one/a single' modifying آفرین, خاست is past 'arose', and نو is an adjective 'new' modifying the acclamation. Word order is typical Persian (adj/quantifier + noun + verb).
segments.s099-g31: باید اکنون is a present‑necessity construction 'now it is necessary / there must now'. شهی is used as 'a king/royal one' and ز تخم کیان means 'from the seed/stock of the Kians' (i.e. from that royal line). Word order places the existential/necessitative predicate before the origin phrase.
segments.s099-g32: به تخت کیی is a goal phrase 'to/onto the Kayan (کیی) throne'. بر کمر بر میان is a terse, poetic grouping meaning roughly 'with the girdle about the waist, in the middle' (بر کمر = 'upon the waist' i.e. bound about the waist; میان = 'middle/in the midst'). Prepositions and short phrases are compacted for poetic effect.

sentences.s099-s1: He struck the bead in the cup upon the elephant's back; from it a sound rose for several miles.
sentences.s099-s2: The drum's roaring together with the trumpet-blast — those same ragged elephants and the Indian retainers.
sentences.s099-s3: From Zabulistan arose an uprising; it struck a cry to the sleeping earth: 'Rise!'
sentences.s099-s4: Rostam the champion advanced into the inner chamber; after him, like an old man, (came) the others.
sentences.s099-s5: Such a commotion came from the army in the gates, the plains, and the rāgh that a crow could not alight on any head.
sentences.s099-s6: They struck with axes and so swept away the place; in the world there was neither head to be seen nor foot.
sentences.s099-s7: When the rose-garden came into bloom, he brought an army from Zabulistan.
sentences.s099-s8: Afrasiab received news about Zal; he rose from his rest and from eating and sleeping.
sentences.s099-s17: A king who has a wine‑tinged colour, so that the human body is not headless.
sentences.s099-s18: The mobad showed me a sign in the time of a certain king, one endowed with farr and youthful fortune.
sentences.s099-s19: From the seed of Fereydun, the hero Kay Qobad — he who, with farr and exaltation and with counsel and justice…
sentences.s099-s9: He brought the army toward Khwar of Rey and set it down on a meadow there that had poor water and reeds.
sentences.s099-s10: From Iran the host came continually, by the desert road toward the battlefield.
sentences.s099-s11: There remained two parasangs between the armies; the spahbed summoned the seasoned men.
sentences.s099-s12: To them he said thus: “O wise ones, worldly‑wise and experienced riders,”
sentences.s099-s13: Here too I have arrayed this army; I sought great rulership and a moon‑like splendor.
sentences.s099-s14: The opinion spread far and wide that the king was without a throne; all affairs lacked direction and the host was leaderless.
sentences.s099-s15: When the auspicious one sat upon the throne, a new acclamation arose from the world.
sentences.s099-s16: Now a king must be (raised) from the seed of the Kians and set upon the Kayan throne, his girdle about his waist in the middle.
